target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 1 protocol foundation
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR is the narrow foundation. It creates the versioned broker SDK subpath and bundled channel-broker plugin, but it does not move Telegram, Discord, Slack, or any user defaults. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 1 - SDK and bundled broker foundation - Role: Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract. - Head: `6c0b6c2567` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `6c0b6c2567` Focused local validation for this phase is documented below. Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. Native channel defaults remain unchanged unless this phase explicitly says otherwise. ## Summary Adds Phase 1 of the Channel Broker consolidation program: a versioned `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` protocol surface, a bundled `channel-broker` channel plugin, provider-owned outbound request/receipt helpers, docs, and focused tests. This PR intentionally stays at the protocol/foundation layer. Telegram, Discord, Slack, and later channel migrations are tracked separately and should not be folded into this PR. ## Linked Context Closes #86107 Related #86105 Related #86108 Related #86109 Related #86110 Related #86111 Related #86112 Related #86113 Related #86114 Related #86115 ## Review Repair History The current Phase 1 branch includes focused repairs for ClawSweeper's blocker classes: - Preserves native prefix ownership when `channel-broker` declares platform aliases that overlap native plugins. If Telegram/Discord/Slack are registered, their prefixes stay native-owned; broker aliases own a platform prefix only when no native plugin declares it. - Adds the standard channel SecretRef contract for `channels.channel-broker.{outboundToken,signingSecret}` plus `accounts.*` and `providers.*` variants, and changes the broker schema/docs to use SecretRef-compatible credential input. - Threads `AbortSignal` through broker outbound requests, including the HTTP `fetch` transport and custom runtime override path. ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Verified the Phase 1 broker surface resolves as a bundled channel and produces a normalized V1 outbound request for a Telegram-scoped broker target. This proves the user-visible foundation being shipped in this PR: catalog discovery, channel id normalization, target construction, protocol versioning, platform normalization, thread preservation, and final-send payload shape. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, branch `feat/channel-broker-protocol`, after the current Phase 1 repair/doc series through `d39d47b3db`. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ```bash node --import tsx -e 'const { listBundledChannelCatalogEntries } = await import("./src/channels/bundled-channel-catalog-read.ts"); const { normalizeChatChannelId } = await import("./src/channels/ids.ts"); const { buildBrokerConversationTarget, createBrokerOutboundRequest } = await import("./src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts"); const catalog = listBundledChannelCatalogEntries().find((entry) => entry.id === "channel-broker"); const target = buildBrokerConversationTarget({ platform: "Telegram", conversationId: "chat 123", threadId: "topic-7" }); const request = createBrokerOutboundRequest({ requestId: "send-proof-1", providerId: "synthetic-provider", platform: "Telegram", accountId: "default", conversation: { id: "chat 123", type: "channel", threadId: "topic-7" }, mode: "final", payloads: [{ text: "broker proof" }], requirements: { text: true, thread: true } }); console.log(JSON.stringify({ discoveredChannel: catalog && { id: catalog.id, order: catalog.order, docsPath: catalog.channel.docsPath }, channelNormalization: normalizeChatChannelId("channel-broker"), target, outboundProtocolVersion: request.version, outboundMode: request.mode, outboundPlatform: request.platform, outboundConversation: request.conversation, outboundPayloadText: request.payloads[0]?.text }, null, 2));' ``` - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output copied from the command above: ```json { "discoveredChannel": { "id": "channel-broker", "order": 60, "docsPath": "/channels/channel-broker" }, "channelNormalization": "channel-broker", "target": "telegram:chat%20123?threadId=topic-7", "outboundProtocolVersion": 1, "outboundMode": "final", "outboundPlatform": "telegram", "outboundConversation": { "id": "chat 123", "type": "channel", "threadId": "topic-7" }, "outboundPayloadText": "broker proof" } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** OpenClaw discovered the bundled `channel-broker` entry, normalized the channel id, built a Telegram broker target that preserved `threadId`, and emitted a V1 final outbound request with normalized `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.threadId: "topic-7"`, and payload text `broker proof`. - **What was not tested:** No real third-party broker provider delivery was tested in Phase 1. That is intentionally deferred to the Phase 2 migration proof issues for Telegram, Discord, and Slack. ## Focused Local Validation All local validation below ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: ```bash git diff --check ``` ```bash node node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 --config .oxfmtrc.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/runtime.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/types.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts ``` ## Known Inherited Gates - `pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check` has an inherited clean-upstream SDK budget drift; this PR adds the intended `plugin-sdk/channel-broker` public subpath. - `pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary` has inherited unrelated glossary-label failures on clean upstream; Channel Broker glossary entries were added in this branch.

target.title: fix(auto-reply): strip reasoning blocks before NO_REPLY detection (#66701)
target.body:
## Summary

`isSilentReplyText` in `src/auto-reply/tokens.ts` uses an exact-match regex (`^\s*NO_REPLY\s*$`) to suppress agent replies. Some models can prepend reasoning blocks before the token:

```text
<think>
Cav is talking to someone else. I will stay quiet here.
</think>NO_REPLY
```

The regex fails on this input and the raw reasoning text is posted to chat.

This patch calls `stripReasoningTagsFromText` from `src/shared/text/reasoning-tags.ts` before testing the input. That strips supported reasoning tags such as `<think>`, `<thinking>`, and `<antthinking>` before the exact `NO_REPLY` match.

The #19537 behavior is covered by tests: substantive replies that end with `NO_REPLY` are still not suppressed after reasoning-tag stripping.

Closes #66701.

## Changes

- `src/auto-reply/tokens.ts` — import `stripReasoningTagsFromText` and strip reasoning tags before the exact-match check in `isSilentReplyText`
- `src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts` — 7 new test cases for reasoning-prefix variants, multiple blocks, token-inside-block, and unclosed-block cases

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** `isSilentReplyText("<think>I will stay quiet.</think>NO_REPLY")` returned `false`, causing reasoning text to be posted to chat. After this patch it returns `true`.

**Real environment tested:** Unit test suite `src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts`

**Exact steps:**
```
pnpm test src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

**Evidence (branch-local run):**
```
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > isSilentReplyText > returns true for exact token 2ms

 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > isSilentReplyText > returns true for mixed-case token 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > isSilentReplyText > returns false for undefined/empty 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > isSilentReplyText > returns false for substantive text ending with token (#19537) 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > isSilentReplyText > returns false for substantive text starting with token 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > isSilentReplyText > returns false for token embedded in text 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > isSilentReplyText > returns true when <think> block precedes token (#66701) 1ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > isSilentReplyText > returns true when <think> block with whitespace precedes token (#66701) 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > isSilentReplyText > returns true for <antthinking> block prefix (#66701) 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > isSilentReplyText > returns false when substantive text follows token after reasoning strip (#66701) 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > isSilentReplyText > returns true for multiple sequential reasoning blocks before token (#66701) 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > isSilentReplyText > returns false when token is inside a reasoning block with content after (#66701) 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > isSilentReplyText > returns false for unclosed reasoning block containing substantive text before token (#66701) 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > stripSilentToken > strips token from end of text 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > stripSilentToken > does not strip token from start of text 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > stripSilentToken > strips token with emoji (#30916) 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > stripSilentToken > does not strip embedded token suffix without whitespace delimiter 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > stripSilentToken > strips only trailing occurrence 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > stripSilentToken > returns empty string when only token remains 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > stripSilentToken > strips token preceded by bold markdown formatting 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > custom silent tokens > handles custom token for 'exact-token detection' 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > custom silent tokens > handles custom token for 'substantive text detection' 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > custom silent tokens > handles custom token for 'trailing token stripping' 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > stripLeadingSilentToken > strips glued leading token text 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > startsWithSilentToken > matches leading glued silent tokens case-insensitively 1ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > startsWithSilentToken > rejects separated substantive prefixes and exact-token-only text 1ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > isSilentReplyPrefixText > matches uppercase token lead fragments 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > isSilentReplyPrefixText > rejects ambiguous natural-language prefixes 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > isSilentReplyPrefixText > keeps underscore guard for non-NO_REPLY tokens 0ms
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts > isSilentReplyPrefixText > rejects non-prefixes and mixed characters 0ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  31 passed (31)
   Start at  20:25:27
   Duration  274ms (transform 84ms, setup 0ms, import 104ms, tests 10ms, environment 0ms)
```

**Observed result:** All 31 tests pass including the 7 new `#66701` cases that cover `<think>`, `<antthinking>`, multiple blocks, token-inside-block, and unclosed-block variants.

**What was not tested:** Live Telegram, Signal, or Slack delivery with a real Gemini or Haiku model producing a reasoning-prefixed `NO_REPLY`. That path requires a running OpenClaw instance with a reasoning model configured.

target.title: fix(agents): count exec-path openclaw cron add in successfulCronAdds
target.body:
### What

This PR fixes an issue where the `successfulCronAdds` counter was not incremented when an agent ran `openclaw cron add` as a shell command via `exec` or `bash`.

### Why

The existing logic only checked for the structured `cron` tool. This caused the agent runner to misread the counter and incorrectly append "I did not schedule a reminder" to the transcript after a successful shell-based cron addition.

### How

An `else if` block is added to `pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts` to inspect `exec`/`bash` results. It uses an anchored regex (`/^\s*openclaw\s+cron\s+add\b/i`) to identify the command and requires a success receipt (`created|scheduled`) in the output before incrementing the counter.

This approach incorporates the hardened regex requested in the prior (self-closed) PR #53024. New regression tests are included to cover the fix and prevent bypasses.

Closes #52972

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Agent runner incorrectly appends "I did not schedule a reminder" after the agent runs `openclaw cron add` via exec/bash shell tool (structured `cron` tool path was unaffected).

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw development clone, Node 24, Linux (WSL2). Exec/bash tool path exercised via the handler directly.

**Exact steps:**
```
pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

**Evidence (branch-local run):**
```
 ✓ |agents| src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts > handleToolExecutionStart read path checks > does not warn when read tool uses file_path alias 6ms
 ✓ |agents| src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts > handleToolExecutionStart read path checks > warns when read tool has neither path nor file_path 1ms
 ✓ |agents| src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts > handleToolExecutionStart read path checks > awaits onBlockReplyFlush before continuing tool start processing 2ms
 ✓ |agents| src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts > handleToolExecutionEnd cron.add commitment tracking > increments successfulCronAdds when cron add succeeds 2ms
 ✓ |agents| src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts > handleToolExecutionEnd cron.add commitment tracking > does not increment successfulCronAdds when cron add fails 1ms
 ✓ |agents| src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts > handleToolExecutionEnd cron.add commitment tracking > increments successfulCronAdds when exec runs openclaw cron add and output confirms creation 1ms

target.title: fix(agent-job): surface pending error snapshot when outer timeout fires
target.body:
### What

Fixes a race condition in `waitForAgentJob` that caused pending agent errors to be discarded when the primary timeout fired.

### Why

When an agent job fails, an `error` event schedules the job to finish with an error snapshot after a 15-second grace period. If the outer `timeoutMs` for `waitForAgentJob` fires before this grace period elapses, it calls `finish(null)`, canceling the pending error.

The caller receives a generic timeout message instead of the actual root cause, making debugging difficult.

### How

The outer timeout callback now checks for a pending error via `getPendingAgentRunError()` before resolving with `null`. This function reads from a module-level map that is populated as soon as the `error` event is received, guaranteeing that the snapshot is available if the race occurs.

A new regression test using fake timers (`5s < 15s`) confirms the fix.

Closes #52497

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Race condition in `waitForAgentJob` — outer timeout fires `finish(null)`, discarding the pending error snapshot. Caller gets a generic timeout instead of the actual agent failure reason.

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw development clone, Node 24, Linux (WSL2). Gateway `waitForAgentJob` path exercised directly with controlled timing.

**Exact steps:**
```
pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

**Observed result:** All 61 tests pass. The new regression test (`surfaces pending error snapshot when outer timeout fires before error grace period`) passed and covers the pending-error-snapshot path when the outer timeout fires before the error grace period.

**What was not tested:** Race with sub-100ms timeouts; concurrent multi-job error races; production OpenClaw load; error snapshots arriving after the outer timeout resolves.

target.title: fix(codex): cap managed app-server trace logs
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes/ref: #86214

Adds startup retention for OpenClaw-managed Codex app-server trace logs under `CODEX_HOME`:

- applies before shared and isolated Codex app-server startup
- rotates `logs_2.sqlite`, `logs_2.sqlite-wal`, and `logs_2.sqlite-shm` when their combined size exceeds the cap
- defaults to a 512 MiB cap and keeps the 2 newest retired snapshots
- exposes env controls for disable/max-bytes/snapshot count
- logs a warning when rotation happens, but does not fail app-server startup if retention itself fails

This deliberately bounds OpenClaw-managed storage without needing to know the native Codex SQLite schema.

## Why

In one affected OpenClaw install, the managed Codex DB grew to ~43 GiB / 14.5M rows in under 8 days, while standalone Codex on the same machine had only an ~81 MiB `~/.codex/logs_2.sqlite`.

The live process chain was:

`openclaw gateway -> codex.js app-server -> native codex app-server --listen stdio://`

The growing file was:

`~/.openclaw/agents/main/agent/codex-home/logs_2.sqlite`

Additional evidence was posted on #86214:

https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/86214#issuecomment-4530473714

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** OpenClaw-managed Codex app-server log storage must not grow without a bounded retention path when `logs_2.sqlite` accumulates high-volume response/WebSocket/SSE trace rows.
- **Real environment tested:** Affected local macOS OpenClaw install on the same machine that produced the ~43 GiB `~/.openclaw/agents/main/agent/codex-home/logs_2.sqlite`; standalone Codex on the same machine was only ~81 MiB.
- **Exact steps or command run after fix:** Applied the local retention mitigation for the affected OpenClaw-managed Codex home, then captured the live retention state with `bin/openclaw-storage-retention --json --apply-safe` and `state/storage-retention/latest.json`. This PR upstreams the app-server startup cap so OpenClaw-managed Codex homes get a bounded version of the same protection before startup.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from the affected OpenClaw setup after the retention run:

```json
{
  "status": "ok",
  "attention_required": false,
  "before_size_gib": 0.733,
  "after_size_gib": 0.733,
  "remaining_old_rows": null,
  "deleted_rows": 2544,
  "compact_status": "skipped",
  "diagnosis_path": "/Users/.../.openclaw/workspace/state/storage-retention/codex-log-summary-20260524T225926Z.json"
}
```

Earlier in the same incident, pruning and VACUUM brought the OpenClaw-managed Codex DB from ~43 GiB / ~14.5M rows down to ~678 MiB with `remaining_old_rows=0`. The follow-up retention run above shows the same real OpenClaw setup stayed healthy and only had 2,544 additional expired rows to delete.

- **Observed result after fix:** The affected OpenClaw install no longer has old Codex log rows accumulating past the retention boundary, compaction was not needed on the follow-up run, and the watchdog reported `status: ok` / `attention_required: false`.
- **What was not tested:** This PR does not reduce native Codex trace volume by category, and I did not replace the production OpenClaw gateway binary with this branch on the Mac mini. The patch bounds OpenClaw-managed `CODEX_HOME` storage at app-server startup; native trace downsampling can be a separate follow-up.

## Config

- `OPENCLAW_CODEX_APP_SERVER_LOG_RETENTION=0|false|off|no` disables retention
- `OPENCLAW_CODEX_APP_SERVER_LOG_MAX_BYTES=<positive integer>` overrides the default cap
- `OPENCLAW_CODEX_APP_SERVER_RETIRED_LOG_SNAPSHOTS=<non-negative integer>` overrides retained retired snapshots

## Tests

- `pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/codex/src/app-server/log-retention.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/log-retention.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/shared-client.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`
- `pnpm test:extension codex -- extensions/codex/src/app-server/log-retention.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/shared-client.test.ts`

## Follow-up

This does not reduce the native Codex app-server's trace volume by category. It prevents unbounded disk growth for OpenClaw-managed Codex homes. A separate follow-up could add native trace throttling or category-level filtering if maintainers want a smaller steady-state DB.

target.title: fix(agents): log warnings instead of swallowing subagent errors
target.body:
## Problem

Two catch blocks in the subagent registry silently discard errors, leaving no diagnostic trail when hook emission or disk restore fails:

1. **`emitSubagentEndedHookOnce`** (`subagent-registry-completion.ts:116`): `catch { return false; }` discards hook-runner errors when emitting the `subagent_ended` lifecycle hook.
2. **`restoreSubagentRunsOnce`** (`subagent-registry.ts:645`): `catch { // ignore restore failures }` discards errors when restoring subagent runs from disk on cold start.

When either path fails (hook runner unavailable, corrupt disk state, reconciliation error), the system continues silently. Operators have no indication that subagent lifecycle hooks were skipped or that restored runs were lost.

## Fix

Wire `log.warn()` into both catch blocks so errors are logged before the existing fallback behavior continues:

1. **Hook emission path**: `catch (err) { log.warn("failed to emit subagent_ended hook for run ${runId}: ..."); return false; }`
2. **Restore path**: `catch (err) { log.warn("failed to restore subagent runs from disk: ..."); }`

Both paths still return/continue as before (no behavior change beyond logging). The completion file gets its own `createSubsystemLogger("agents/subagent-registry-completion")` to match the existing `createSubsystemLogger("agents/subagent-registry")` in the registry file.

Production diff is +8 lines changed across 2 files.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Two silent `catch` blocks in the subagent registry discard errors during hook emission and disk restore. The fix adds `log.warn()` to both paths so failures leave a diagnostic trail instead of being silently swallowed.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, OpenClaw built from source (commit 1d5b5db) at `/tmp/fix-82943`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
Step 1. Build OpenClaw from patched source
cd /tmp/fix-82943
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build

Step 2. Verify both log.warn calls are in compiled production code
grep -n "failed to emit subagent_ended hook" dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js
grep -n "failed to restore subagent runs" dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js

Step 3. Start the patched gateway
timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway

Step 4. Run test suites for both changed files
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry-completion.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**Compiled production code verification**

Both `log.warn` calls are present in the compiled subagent-registry bundle:

```console
$ grep -n "failed to emit subagent_ended hook" dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js
94:log$2.warn(`failed to emit subagent_ended hook for run ${runId}: ${err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err)}`);

$ grep -n "failed to restore subagent runs" dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js
1911:log.warn(`failed to restore subagent runs from disk: ${err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err)}`);
```

Line 94 is `emitSubagentEndedHookOnce` (the hook emission path). Line 1911 is `restoreSubagentRunsOnce` (the disk restore path). Before this fix, both were `catch { }` (empty) and `catch { // ignore restore failures }` respectively.

The `log$2` prefix on line 94 is the compiled form of the new `createSubsystemLogger("agents/subagent-registry-completion")`, while `log` on line 1911 is the existing `createSubsystemLogger("agents/subagent-registry")`.

**Live gateway startup proof**

The patched gateway starts, loads the subagent registry with both warning-enabled catch blocks, and runs cleanly:

```console
$ timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway
2026-05-21T12:39:28.862-07:00 [gateway] loading configuration…
2026-05-21T12:39:28.917-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-21T12:39:33.527-07:00 [health-monitor] started (interval: 300s, startup-grace: 60s, channel-connect-grace: 120s)
2026-05-21T12:39:34.421-07:00 [gateway] agent model: openai/gpt-5.5 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
2026-05-21T12:39:34.422-07:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: acpx, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 5.5s)
2026-05-21T12:39:34.913-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T12:39:52.803-07:00 [gateway] signal SIGTERM received
2026-05-21T12:39:52.807-07:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down
2026-05-21T12:39:52.856-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 38ms
```

No `[agents/subagent-registry-completion]` or `[agents/subagent-registry]` warnings appear in the startup log, confirming the catch blocks are not triggered during normal operation (they are defensive logging for error paths only).

**Vitest test suites (supplemental)**

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry-completion.test.ts
 ✓ |agents-core| subagent-registry-completion.test.ts (8 tests) 86ms
 ✓ |agents-support| subagent-registry-completion.test.ts (8 tests) 73ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  16 passed (16)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts
 ✓ |agents-core| subagent-registry.test.ts (29 tests) 2679ms
 ✓ |agents-support| subagent-registry.test.ts (29 tests) 2264ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  58 passed (58)
```

**Fault injection proof: restore catch path fires in real gateway**

The `restoreSubagentRunsOnce` catch handler was triggered in a **live running gateway** by injecting `throw new Error("INJECTED: corrupt registry state")` at the top of the try block in the compiled `dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js`. This forces the catch path to fire during gateway startup.

**Steps:**
1. Back up the compiled subagent-registry bundle.
2. Inject `throw new Error(...)` inside the try block of `restoreSubagentRunsOnce` (after `restoreAttempted = true`, before `restoreSubagentRunsFromDisk`).
3. Start the gateway.
4. Observe the `log.warn` message in the gateway output.
5. Confirm the gateway continues to start normally (the catch swallows the error).
6. Restore the original bundle.

**Terminal output (fault injection):**

```console
2026-05-21T16:15:54.095-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-21T16:15:54.710-07:00 [agents/subagent-registry] failed to restore subagent runs from disk: INJECTED: corrupt registry state
2026-05-21T16:15:54.832-07:00 [gateway] starting HTTP server...
2026-05-21T16:15:56.662-07:00 [gateway] agent model: openai/gpt-5.5 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
2026-05-21T16:15:56.665-07:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins; 2.6s)
2026-05-21T16:15:57.476-07:00 [gateway] ready
```

**Line-by-line analysis:**

- `[gateway] starting...`: Gateway startup begins.
- `[agents/subagent-registry] failed to restore subagent runs from disk: INJECTED: corrupt registry state`: The **`log.warn` in the catch handler fired**. The error message includes the full error text. Before this fix, this catch block was `catch { // ignore restore failures }` with no logging at all.
- `[gateway] ready`: The gateway **continued to start normally** despite the restore failure. The catch handler swallowed the error and let startup proceed.

Without this fix, the same error would be silently ignored (empty catch block with only a comment), leaving no diagnostic trail.

**Fault injection proof: hook emission catch path fires in real gateway**

The `emitSubagentEndedHookOnce` catch handler was also triggered in a **live running gateway**. A conditional `throw` was injected inside the try block of `emitSubagentEndedHookOnce` in `dist/subagent-registry-DLVHJynj.js` (line 73), and a timed trigger called the function after the gateway was ready.

**Terminal output (hook emission catch):**

```console
2026-05-21T21:35:44.608-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T21:35:58.683-07:00 [agents/subagent-registry-completion] failed to emit subagent_ended hook for run INJECTED-PROOF-RUN: INJECTED: hook runner unavailable
2026-05-21T21:35:58.686-07:00 [gateway] signal SIGTERM received
2026-05-21T21:35:58.734-07:00 [gmail-watcher] gmail watcher stopped
2026-05-21T21:35:58.736-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 38ms
```

**Analysis:**
- `[agents/subagent-registry-completion]` subsystem prefix confirms the **new** `createSubsystemLogger("agents/subagent-registry-completion")` is active (this logger was added by this PR).
- `failed to emit subagent_ended hook for run INJECTED-PROOF-RUN: INJECTED: hook runner unavailable` is the exact `log.warn` message from the catch block at compiled line 94.
- The gateway continued running after the hook emission failure (the catch returns `false`, not rethrow).
- Before this fix, this catch block was `catch { return false; }` with no logging at all.

**Both catch blocks are now proven in live gateway runs:**
1. **Restore catch** (`restoreSubagentRunsOnce`): `[agents/subagent-registry] failed to restore subagent runs from disk: INJECTED: corrupt registry state`
2. **Hook emission catch** (`emitSubagentEndedHookOnce`): `[agents/subagent-registry-completion] failed to emit subagent_ended hook for run INJECTED-PROOF-RUN: INJECTED: hook runner unavailable`

Observed result after fix: Both new `log.warn` calls fire correctly in production compiled code. The restore catch logs under `[agents/subagent-registry]` and the hook emission catch logs under `[agents/subagent-registry-completion]`. Both allow the gateway to continue running. All 74 tests pass across both test files.

What was not tested: The hook emission was triggered via injected timer call rather than a real subagent completion event. The injection proves the catch handler mechanism (log.warn + return false + gateway continues) works identically to the restore path proof.

commands.author: stevenepalmer

target.title: fix(ui): reset routed sessions to main on restore
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Control UI could restore a historical routed session key like `agent:*:*:(direct|group):*` from persisted local settings after reload.
- Why it matters: users would reopen Control UI and land in a stale routed session instead of the real main session, making the UI look misrouted even though the backend heartbeat target remained main.
- What changed: session restore now sanitizes persisted routed direct/group session keys during settings load and falls back to `main` instead.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway routing behavior, heartbeat behavior, or live session switching logic was changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #61661
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Control UI restoring historical routed direct/group sessions from persisted local settings instead of falling back to main.
- Real environment tested: local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` on Linux.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm vitest run ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
  - `pnpm tsgo:core`
  - `pnpm lint:core`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
```text
✓ ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts ... falls back to main when restoring a routed direct session
✓ ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts ... falls back to main when restoring a routed group session
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       18 passed (18)
```
- Observed result after fix: persisted `agent:*:*:(direct|group):*` session selections are normalized back to `main` during settings restore.
- What was not tested: explicit session selection through URL query params or live backend routing changes, because this PR only changes local restore behavior.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Control UI storage restore trusted persisted `sessionKey` / `lastActiveSessionKey` values without filtering out historical routed direct/group sessions.
- Missing detection / guardrail: storage tests covered scoped session persistence but did not cover stale routed-session restore behavior.
- Contributing context (if known): main-session normalization already existed later in the gateway boot path, but the bad routed session had already won at the storage restore layer.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: persisted routed `direct`/`group` session keys fall back to `main` on Control UI restore.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug originates in local persisted settings load, so the storage tests are the exact boundary where the regression occurs.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): branch adds two restore-specific cases for routed `direct` and `group` sessions.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

After reload/reopen, Control UI will no longer restore stale routed `direct`/`group` sessions from persisted local settings; it will fall back to the main session instead.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Area: Control UI local storage restore

### Steps

1. Seed persisted Control UI settings with a routed direct/group session key in `sessionsByGateway`.
2. Call `loadSettings()`.
3. Verify restored `sessionKey` / `lastActiveSessionKey` fall back to `main`.

### Expected

- routed historical sessions should not win restore by default
- restored settings should point back to main

### Actual

- both new routed-session storage tests pass
- `tsgo:core` passes
- `lint:core` passes

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the restore sanitization happens in `ui/src/ui/storage.ts`, which is the correct layer for this bug.
- Verified routed `direct` and `group` session keys now restore to `main` in storage tests.
- Verified core type/lint checks pass on the changed files.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a user might intentionally want to reopen a routed direct/group session from persisted state.
  - Mitigation: this PR only changes the automatic restore default; explicit session selection can still be handled through current UI/navigation flows.
- Risk: over-matching could reset legitimate session keys.
  - Mitigation: the sanitization is narrowly limited to `agent:*:*:(direct|group):*` patterns only.



---

## Real behavior proof

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.5 /tmp/pincer-openclaw

 ✓  ui  ../../ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts (22 tests) 38ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  22 passed (22)
    Start at  00:14:43
    Duration  611ms
```

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Routed direct/group session keys persisted in localStorage were not cleared on restore. The regex `/^agent:[^:]+:[^:]+:(direct|group):.+$/` only matched 5-segment per-channel-peer keys, missing `agent:<id>:direct:<peer>` (per-peer) and `agent:<id>:<channel>:<acct>:direct:<peer>` (per-account-channel-peer) shapes emitted by `buildAgentPeerSessionKey`.
**Real environment tested:** Node.js v24.14.0, vitest v4.1.5, jsdom, Linux.
**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts`
**After-fix evidence:** 22/22 tests pass including two new tests covering per-peer (`agent:main:direct:ou_123`) and per-account-channel-peer (`agent:main:feishu:acct_abc:direct:ou_123`) shapes, both correctly falling back to `main`.
**Observed result after fix:** All three `buildAgentPeerSessionKey` direct shapes and group shapes reset to main on storage restore.
**What was not tested:** Live Control UI reload in a browser with actual localStorage entries; all three direct key shapes verified via jsdom storage unit tests.

## Current-head follow-up proof — 2026-05-20

Behavior or issue addressed:
- Covers ClawSweeper's threaded per-peer routed session gap for agent:<id>:direct:<peer>:thread:<id> and legacy dm equivalents.

Real environment tested:
- PR branch fix/control-ui-routed-session-restore at commit 156d49e17c.
- Local Control UI storage module executed through jsdom with real loadSettings() and browser-like localStorage/sessionStorage.
- Full Chromium proof was attempted, but this container's headless Chromium cannot launch because libglib-2.0.so.0 is unavailable; the prior connected-browser proof still covers the Control UI reload path for the earlier provider-scoped routed shape.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
~~~text
COREPACK_HOME=/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/agents/pr-machine/workspace/.corepack pnpm -s vitest run ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts
CI=true COREPACK_HOME=/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/agents/pr-machine/workspace/.corepack pnpm -s check:test-types
git diff --check
node --import tsx <jsdom loadSettings proof script>
~~~

Evidence after fix:
~~~text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  33 passed (33)

tsgo core/extensions test completed successfully
git diff --check completed successfully

{"currentHead":"156d49e17c","seededSession":"agent:main:direct:ou_123:thread:99","loadedGatewayUrl":"wss://gateway.example:8443","restoredSessionKey":"main","restoredLastActiveSessionKey":"main","restoredToMain":true}
{"currentHead":"156d49e17c","seededSession":"agent:main:dm:12345:thread:12345:99","loadedGatewayUrl":"wss://gateway.example:8443","restoredSessionKey":"main","restoredLastActiveSessionKey":"main","restoredToMain":true}
~~~

Observed result after fix:
- Threaded per-peer direct and legacy dm routed session keys now restore to main for both sessionKey and lastActiveSessionKey.

What was not tested:
- A fresh current-head Chromium screenshot was not captured because Playwright's bundled Chromium cannot start in this container due the missing libglib-2.0.so.0 system library.

commands.author: 100yenadmin

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 4 constrained provider capabilities
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR keeps WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles honest by making hosted, device-bound, self-hosted, Mac-hosted, and bridge-backed constraints visible instead of pretending they are equivalent official bot APIs. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 4 - Constrained/device-bound provider capabilities - Role: Adds constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers. - Head: `ea54227d88` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `ea54227d88` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening introduced in #86154 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest stack hardening and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-runtime-api-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-support.config.ts src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts --maxWorkers=1` (targeted verification for the previously timed-out CI file) - `pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-runtime-api-guardrails.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts` - `pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json extensions/channel-broker/runtime-api.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/platforms.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts` - `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` - `pnpm format:docs:check` - `pnpm lint:docs` - `pnpm docs:check-mdx` - `pnpm docs:check-links` - `pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary` skipped locally because this worktree had no merge-base context; GitHub Actions remains the broad docs authority. - `git diff --check` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds Phase 4 constrained/device-bound provider metadata to the Channel Broker contract. - Covers WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles-backed iMessage bridges as broker provider capabilities, not native plugin rewrites. - Stacks on Phase 3 official/app capability matrix (#86164), Phase 2D Slack proof (#86157), Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86114 Related #86105 Related #86108 Supersedes closed PR #86148, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Adds `constraints` and `badges` to broker platform/provider capabilities. - Models constrained provider facts such as: - `businessApi`, `cloudApi`, `providerHosted` - `deviceBound`, `linkedDevice`, `qrPairing`, `sessionFragile` - `selfHosted`, `phoneNumberRequired`, `signalCli` - `macHostRequired`, `messagesSignedIn`, `privateApiOptional`, `privateApiRequired` - `externalBridge` - Exposes resolved provider capabilities in channel-broker status snapshots so UI/status can show constrained-provider badges. - Adds WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage target proof through `channel-broker`. - Documents constrained provider expectations in Channel Broker, SDK, WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles migration docs. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Constrained providers expose hosted, device-bound, self-hosted, Mac-hosted, and bridge-backed limits through broker `constraints`/`badges`, and WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage still route through provider-owned broker requests. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that evaluated constrained-provider capability support and called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` for `broker:whatsapp:direct:15551234567`, `broker:signal:direct:+15551234567`, and `broker:imessage:group:chat-guid-1?threadId=imessage-thread-1`. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "constrainedProviders": { "whatsappBusiness": true, "whatsappDeviceBound": false, "signalDeviceBound": true }, "outbound": [ { "label": "whatsapp-direct", "platform": "whatsapp", "conversation": { "id": "15551234567", "type": "direct" } }, { "label": "signal-direct", "platform": "signal", "conversation": { "id": "+15551234567", "type": "direct" } }, { "label": "imessage-group", "platform": "imessage", "conversation": { "id": "chat-guid-1", "type": "group", "threadId": "imessage-thread-1" } } ] } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live SDK/plugin path distinguished WhatsApp Business-style support from device-bound WhatsApp requirements, accepted Signal's self-hosted/device-bound constraint set, and produced broker provider requests for WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage. - **What was not tested:** Live WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, or BlueBubbles-backed provider traffic; this PR proves the broker contract/schema/docs layer and leaves broad validation to GitHub Actions. ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/platforms.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/status.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/types.ts docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md docs/channels/signal.md docs/channels/imessage.md docs/channels/imessage-from-bluebubbles.md` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md docs/channels/signal.md docs/channels/imessage.md docs/channels/imessage-from-bluebubbles.md` - `pnpm dlx --config.resolution-mode=highest markdownlint-cli2 --config config/markdownlint-cli2.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md docs/channels/signal.md docs/channels/imessage.md docs/channels/imessage-from-bluebubbles.md` ## Review state - Live for maintainer review; no longer draft. - This PR does not implement Phase 5 legacy-plugin freeze. - Next phase: #86115 freezes new legacy native-channel feature work after broker migration paths exist. This PR supplies the constrained-provider metadata needed before that freeze can be credible.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 3 official channel capability matrix
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR prevents the long tail from becoming a new pile of native branches. It expresses official app/API platforms as broker aliases and capability rows first, and it now enforces declared delivery capabilities before outbound handoff. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2D Slack migration proof
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR proves Slack DMs and channel threads can route through broker-owned semantics while native Slack remains the default path. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2D - Slack opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. - Head: `f754a1a1c8` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `f754a1a1c8` This branch also carries a tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation for this phase is documented below. Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. Native channel defaults remain unchanged unless this phase explicitly says otherwise. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2D Slack migration proof for the Channel Broker rollout. - Keeps native Slack default behavior unchanged; this only proves opt-in broker routing for Slack targets. - Stacks on Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86112 Related #86108 Related #86105 Supersedes closed PR #86142, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Normalize broker-prefixed Slack DM targets such as `broker:slack:user:U12345678` into broker provider requests with `conversation.type = "direct"`. - Normalize broker-prefixed Slack channel thread targets such as `broker:slack:channel:C12345678?threadId=1716500000.000001` into broker provider requests with `conversation.type = "channel"` and `threadId`. - Canonicalize Slack broker session routes to the resolved platform target, preserving direct-route semantics as `slack:direct%3A` and thread routes as `slack:?threadId=`. ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Slack DM and threaded-channel targets can route through `channel-broker` while preserving direct/channel conversation semantics and Slack `threadTs` as broker `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:slack:channel:C12345678?threadId=1716500000.000001"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "slack-thread", "messageId": "native-slack-thread", "platform": "slack", "conversation": { "id": "C12345678", "type": "channel", "threadId": "1716500000.000001" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path produced the expected Slack provider request and kept threaded delivery represented by the broker `threadId` field. - **What was not tested:** Live Slack API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` ## Review state - Live for maintainer review; no longer draft. - No native Slack migration is enabled by default in this PR.

## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR proves Telegram topics can move through broker-owned target parsing without changing native Telegram defaults. It is intentionally an opt-in proof layer, not the default migration. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2B - Telegram opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. - Head: `902ea474ac` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `902ea474ac` This branch also carries a tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation for this phase is documented below. Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. Native channel defaults remain unchanged unless this phase explicitly says otherwise. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2B Telegram broker routing proof on top of #86153 and #86096. - Normalizes Telegram topic shorthand in the broker plugin, including `broker:telegram::topic:` and numeric `:` targets. - Canonicalizes outbound broker session routes to `telegram:?threadId=` so topic routing is stable without importing native Telegram internals. ## Rollout Context Closes #86110 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86153 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86137, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. Native Telegram defaults stay unchanged. Existing target-prefix ownership still keeps `telegram:` owned by the native Telegram plugin when that plugin is installed; this PR proves the opt-in broker route through `broker:telegram:...` and the broker alias path when native Telegram is absent. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram topic targets can route through `channel-broker` and become provider requests with canonical `telegram` platform, channel conversation id, and topic `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "telegram-topic", "messageId": "native-telegram-topic", "platform": "telegram", "conversation": { "id": "-1001234567890", "type": "channel", "threadId": "42" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path normalized Telegram topic shorthand into the broker provider request shape without importing native Telegram extension internals. - **What was not tested:** Live Telegram Bot API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. ### Behavior OpenClaw can route a Telegram topic through `channel-broker` without Telegram extension code. The broker receives a normalized platform `telegram`, conversation id, and thread id; OpenClaw keeps the session route canonical and leaves native Telegram prefix ownership intact. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing` - Head: `902ea474ac` (current Telegram proof head) ### Steps 1. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42` through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. 2. Verified the provider request used `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.id: "-1001234567890"`, and `conversation.threadId: "42"`. 3. Resolved the outbound session route for the same target. 4. Verified the route canonicalized to `telegram:-1001234567890?threadId=42` and preserved channel chat semantics. ### Evidence - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 2 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result Telegram topic routing is represented by broker-owned target normalization and provider request semantics, not by core importing or calling native Telegram extension internals. ### Not Tested - Live Telegram Bot API traffic; this PR is the opt-in broker migration proof, not a live provider connector. - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.
